2003-07-01.title: New Premier Of Quebec Keeping Vow To Cut Back
2003-07-01.abstract: Quebec Premier Jean Charest, vowing change in provincial government, has cut cabinet by one-third, cut taxes on small businesses, slashed spending and promised to cut income taxes by 27 percent over next five years; engineered Liberals' landslide defeat of Parti Quebecois by playing down old debate over sovereignty and promising to cut government sloth (M)

2003-07-01.title: World Briefing | Europe: Italy: Immunity Law Appealed
2003-07-01.abstract: Law passed giving Prime Minister Silvio Berlusconi immunity from prosecution is appealed by judges trying his bribery case in Milan; Italy's Supreme Court will rule on law's constitutionality (S)

2003-07-01.title: Anti-U.S. Edict in Iraq
2003-07-01.abstract: Article noted on Iraqi Shiite cleric's condemnation of US-sponsored constitutional council

2003-07-01.title: World Briefing: Europe
2003-07-01.abstract: BRITAIN: Rights For Gay Couples; ITALY: Immunity Law Appealed; FRANCE: Muslim Withdraws Resignation; TURKEY: Reform Vetoed; CZECH REPUBLIC: Official Hired Killer;.

2003-07-01.title: Analysts See Tension in China Within the Top Leadership
2003-07-01.abstract: China analysts say conflict between supporters of Pres Hu Jintao and predecessor Jiang Zemin exposes tensions within top leadership and makes it more difficult for new generation to put its stamp on politics; Jiang, 76, retains enormous political muscle by keeping allies in key posts and staying on as chairman of Central Military Commission; Hu, 60, solidifies his stature with strong handling of SARS epidemic, but has to navigate between liberal intellectuals calling bluntly for political change and Jiang allies, who fear instability and reportedly try to reverse trend toward government openness (M)

2003-07-02.title: Travel Industry Fears New Rules Could Deter Visits to U.S.
2003-07-02.abstract: Travel Industry Assn warns that new security measures intended to prevent terrorists from entering US will likely deter foreign tourists, who will have to submit to interviews and carry computer-readable passports (M)

2003-07-02.title: China's Leader Gives No Sign of Changes to Come
2003-07-02.abstract: Pres Hu Jintao says in major address that 'foremost political duty' of China's leaders is to carry out ideological theories of former Pres Jiang Zemin; tells meeting on Communist Party's 82nd anniversary that members must rededicate themselves to helping urban and rural poor; photo (M)

2003-07-02.title: A U.N. Official to Visit Iran Over Nuclear Sites
2003-07-02.abstract: Dr Mohamed ElBaradei, who heads International Atomic Energy Agency, will visit Iran, which is under mounting international pressure to open up its nuclear program (S)

2003-07-02.title: French Court Orders Release of Mujahedeen Leader
2003-07-02.abstract: In a setback to France's war against terrorism, a Paris appeals court ordered the release on bail of a leader of a long-established armed Iranian opposition group.

2003-07-02.title: Bush Says Attacks on U.S. Forces Won't Deter Him From the Rebuilding of Iraq
2003-07-02.abstract: Pres Bush vows to stay with job of stabilizing and rebuilding Iraq despite continuing attacks on US forces, speech to military personnel at White House; blames former Baath Party and security officials and says there will be no return to tyranny; US is also pressing to increase allied contributions to occupation force (M)

2003-07-03.title: Delay by U.N. On Rebuking North Korea Is Urged
2003-07-03.abstract: China and Russia seek to delay Security Council condemnation of North Korea's nuclear arms program one day after top North Korean general says any sanctions or blockades initiated by United States would be considered 'complete breach' of truce that ended hostilities 50 years ago and would trigger 'merciless retaliatory measures' against South Korean population; North Korean Foreign Min Paek Nam Sun issued similar warning in his letter to Security Council last week, but tone of his letter contrasts sharply with harsh tone of general's follow-up letter; this leads some diplomats to wonder if there is internal North Korean dispute over what mix of conciliatory language and threats should be used with UN and US; one Asian diplomat notes how unusual it is to have Army communicating with UN (M)

2003-07-03.title: Canada law lifts Asian gays
2003-07-03.abstract: HONG KONG:—  While homosexual acts are illegal in many Asian jurisdictions, gay rights activists hope that Canada's new same-sex marriage law will open an avenue for pressuring legal change in the region.

2003-07-03.title: World Briefing: Europe
2003-07-03.abstract: NORTHERN IRELAND; GREECE: Sweeping Party Changes;.

2003-07-03.title: Europe Acts to Require Labeling of Genetically Altered Food
2003-07-03.abstract: European Parliament approves legislation to require strict labels for food and feed made with genetically altered ingredients; move is hailed by environmentalists but pilloried by American farmers; legislation is intended to better inform wary European consumers; it requires supermarkets to label all food containing more than 0.9 percent of genetically modified organism; it also ensures that such food will be traced from moment of its inception to its arrival in European Union through processing stage and into supermarkets; new law is expected to receive final approval by European Union's 15 member states this fall, and to take effect early next year; Bush administration criticizes bill, saying it would be burdensome for food producers, could prejudice consumers against genetically modified food and become barrier to free trade (M)

2003-07-03.title: U.S. Attributes Explosion at Iraqi Mosque to Bomb-Making Activity
2003-07-03.abstract: United States Central Command in Iraq says explosion at mosque in Falluja that killed at least six people, including mosque's imam, resulted from presence of bomb-making class inside; American commander says that unless troops are fired on from mosque, he will continue to adhere to policy of not searching them because it might risk further alienating Muslim population already seething at American-led occupation; members of Falluja mosque insist explosion was caused by American missile fired from helicopter or airplane; their version of events gains currency on streets of Falluja and beyond, prompting vows of revenge; neighbors of mosque say they heard no aircraft before explosion but they did hear that explosives were being stored at mosque; photo; map (M)

2003-07-04.title: Russian Oil Man Arrested; Allies Blame Politics
2003-07-04.abstract: Russian authorities arrest Platon Lebedev, chairman of Menatep, company that owns 61 percent of giant Yukos Oil Co, on charges of embezzling money from state company in 1994; arrest, at hospital where Lebedev was convalescing, is first of major tycoon since Vladimor Gusinsky in 2000, as Pres Vladimir Putin consolidated power; arrest may be blunt message to Yukos chief executive Mikhail B Khodorkovsky to stop involving himself in opposition politics (M)

2003-07-04.title: Bush Insists Liberian President Go Before Troops Come In
2003-07-04.abstract: Pers Bush, enmeshed in internal debate within administration on whether to send US peackeeping troops to Liberia, strongly implices that no soldiers will be deployed until Pres Charles Taylor leaves, interview with African reporters; US military officials meet in Ghana with West African representatives; photo of demonstrators in Monrovia (M)

2003-07-04.title: U.S. 'Still at War,' General Declares; G.I. Dies; 20 Hurt
2003-07-04.abstract: The U.S. also announced a reward of up to $25 million for the capture of Saddam Hussein or confirmation of his death.

2003-07-04.title: Iraq Museum Reopens With Assyrian Treasures
2003-07-04.abstract: Iraqi National Museum re-opens with display of treasures of Nimrud, found after war in submerged bank vault; Col Matthew Bogdanos says looting reports were greatly exaggerated; says that 12,000 objects stolen were mostly items like pottery shards and individual beads, that 42 display-quality objects were looted and ten have been recovered and that most precious collection of coins and seals were untouched because thieves dropped keys in dark (M)

2003-07-04.title: After Tour, Senators Warn U.S. Is Spread Thin in Iraq
2003-07-04.abstract: A bipartisan group of senators, returning from Iraq, warned that American troops are stretched thin and face continuing attack.

2003-07-05.title: World Briefing | Europe: Germany: Jewish Museum A Target
2003-07-05.abstract: Suspected terrorist Shadi Mohd Mustafa Abdellah states during his Dusseldorf trial that he was part of four person group planning attacks on Jewish targets in Dusseldorf and on Jewish Museum in Berlin (Germany); Palestinian from Jordan, he trained with Al Qaeda, but contends he was working with Al Tawhid, terrorist group (S)

2003-07-05.title: Hong Kong's Chief to Amend Internal Security Legislation
2003-07-05.abstract: Hong Kong chief executive Tung Chee-hwa says he will remove or amend several of most controversial provisions of unpopular internal security bill, but not delay legislative consideration of bill; gambles that he can push through bill despite calls for delay by even one of two pro-government parties; bill calls for long jail sentences for people involved in sedition, secession and treason (M)

2003-07-05.title: World Briefing | Europe: Russia: Prosecutors Quiz Oil Magnate
2003-07-05.abstract: Platon Lebedev, chairman of Menatep, which owns 61% of Yukos, Russia's largest oil company, is arrested and his colleague Mikhail B Khodorkovsky is interrogated about alledged embezzlement; claims charges are false; photo (S)

2003-07-05.title: At Hussein's Palace, a Day for Hot Dogs
2003-07-05.abstract: American soldiers and civilians mark Independence Day with party on grounds of Saddam Hussein's Republican Palace, Baghdad; photo (M)

2003-07-05.title: A Recorded Voice, Said to Be Hussein's, Calls for Resistance
2003-07-05.abstract: Arab satellite channel Al Jazeera broadcasts part of audiotape with voice claiming to be that of toppled Iraqi dictator Saddam Hussein urging Iraqis to resist American and British invaders; tape comes as fighting intensifies further in Iraq and day after Bush administration offered $25 million reward for Hussein's capture; voice exhorts Iraqis to protect fighters attacking American and British forces and avoid cooperating with occupation authorities; American officials say troops killed 11 Iraqis that tried to ambush them on road near Balad; map; photo (M)

2003-07-06.title: Turkey Says U.S. Has Detained Some of Its Soldiers in North Iraq
2003-07-06.abstract: Turkey protests in unusually harsh language what it calls detention of 11 Turkish soldiers by American forces in Sulaimaniya, northern Iraq, where there are signs Turks want to play more aggressive role; Prime Min Recep Tayyip Erdogan says Americans have released some of soldiers; scores incident; Turkish newspaper reports Americans acted on intelligence reports that Turkish commandos were plotting to assassinate Kurdish governor of nearby Kirkuk; Turkish officials deny claim; Min Abdullah Gul phones Sec Colin L Powell; hundreds of Turkish troops have operated in northern Iraq since mid-1990's, mainly to hunt down Kurdish guerrillas (M)

2003-07-06.title: Explosions at a Moscow Rock Festival Kill at Least 16
2003-07-06.abstract: Two blasts rip through entrances to suburban Moscow rock festial, killing at least 16 people and wounding up to 60 more; Russian government blames female suicide bombers; at least one reportedly is identified as Chechen; blasts are first major terrorist act in Moscow since last Oct; seem to fit growing pattern of suicide bombing attacks on both government and civilian targets in and around Chechnya; map; photos (M)

2003-07-06.title: Leader Presses Security Bill In Hong Kong After Changes
2003-07-06.abstract: Government and democracy advocates brace for another showdown over internal security bill as Hong Kong chief executive Tung Chee-hwa softens bill but insists legislature pass it quickly; democracy suppporters call changes inadequate and vow to stop bill; China's national legislature urges Hong Kong to approve bill on time; Tung deleted provisions that would have allowed search and seizure without warrant during national security probes and let government ban groups subordinate to groups banned in China on security grounds; photo (M)

2003-07-06.title: Putin Cancels Trip and Vows to Find Attackers
2003-07-06.abstract: The moves came a day after two female suicide bombers killed themselves and at least 13 others at a Moscow rock festival.

2003-07-06.title: In Africa, Bush to Focus on Threats and Opportunities
2003-07-06.abstract: President Bush will leave Monday night for a five-nation tour of Africa, no longer a place that can be left at the bottom of the foreign policy to-do list.

2003-07-07.title: In Blow to Paris, Corsica Rejects French Restructuring Plan
2003-07-07.abstract: Corsicans vote by slim margin to reject referendum intended to enhance their decision-making powers; Pres Jacques Chirac had supported referendum, calling it best way for Corsicans to affirm their attachment to France; few voters seem to understand purpose of exercise or whether outcome would improve their lives; many believe timing of arrest of Corsican nationalist who is most wanted man in France was not accidental, but crass political move intended to garner more positive votes; map (M)

2003-07-07.title: British Panel Clears Blair of Charges of Doctoring Iraq Data
2003-07-07.abstract: A committee said the report resulted in Prime Minister Tony Blair's unknowingly misinforming the House of Commons on reasons for going to war.

2003-07-07.title: Orangemen On Parade Peacefully
2003-07-07.abstract: Annual parade by Protestant Orange Order passes without incident for first time since 1995 in countryside outside Portadown, Northern Ireland; marchers are prevented for sixth consecutive year from walking their traditional route through Roman Catholic neighborhood; photo (S)

2003-07-07.title: 3 G.I.'s Killed In Iraq Capital, One at Campus
2003-07-07.abstract: Three American soldiers are killed in 12-hour period in Baghdad amid growing signs of guerrilla resistance to American forces; deaths represent 26th, 27th and 28th American soldiers to be killed in hostile action since May 1, when Pres Bush said major fighting had ended; in first of latest attacks, American soldier accompanying United States officials visiting Baghdad Univ is fatally shot by unidentified gunman; this raises questions about whether movements of American officials are being tracked by Iraqi insurgents; military officials are describing recent attacks as isolated work of desperate Iraqis connected to toppled government of Saddam Hussein; Gen Richard B Myers, chairman of Joint Chiefs of Staff, says officials will examine whether attacks are becoming better organized; military officials in Baghdad say 7-day effort to root out Iraqi resistance and paramilitary activity in central Iraq has resulted in detention of 282 people and seizure of many weapons; photo; map (M)

2003-07-07.title: Iran Confirms Test of Missile That Is Able to Hit Israel
2003-07-07.abstract: The missile was first tested in 1998 and has a range of 810 miles, which means it can reach Israel and U.S. troops in Saudi Arabia.

2003-07-08.title: Water and Electricity in Baghdad Are Still Below Prewar Levels, Officials Say
2003-07-08.abstract: Two officials overseeing American-led effort to rebuild Iraq say electricity and drinking water available in Baghdad and some other parts of country remain below prewar levels; assessment runs counter to Pentagon's earlier assurances that goal levels for improving services are almost met; it also reflects damage done by looters and saboteurs since end of major combat two months ago (M)

2003-07-08.title: World Briefing | Europe: Ireland: Once Scorned, Now Mayor
2003-07-08.abstract: Tuam (Ireland) Town Council selects Martin Ward, who was once Traveler, also known as tinker, to serve as mayor; he is first mayor appointed from this nomadic ethnic group (S)

2003-07-08.title: Bush Opens Africa Trip With Denunciation of Slavery
2003-07-08.abstract: The president also pledged to work more closely with African nations, but the limits of his promise were immediately on display.

2003-07-08.title: AIDS-Afflicted Villagers Say Chinese Police Attacked Them
2003-07-08.abstract: Residents of Xiongqiao, AIDS-afflicted village in central China, say police raided village, rampaging through homes, beating residents and arresting 13 people; say attack was apparently in retaliation for villagers' previous protests calling for better medical care; raid is largest such clash between officials and residents in rural Henan Province, where AIDS is claiming growing numbers of lives and medical care is minimal; Xiongqiao is one of many villages in province experiencing AIDS epidemic caused by government-induced blood trading in 1990's; map (M)

2003-07-08.title: Berlusconi Expresses Regret for Remarks About German
2003-07-08.abstract: The prime minister of Italy, who last week compared a German parliamentarian to a Nazi, made a peace offering today.

2003-07-09.title: For a Town Council in Iraq, Many Queries, Few Answers
2003-07-09.abstract: Abu Ghraib town council, first to be freely chosen in Iraq since fall of Saddam Hussein, hears litany of complaints about electric power, water, security and the like, and offers usual mix of limited help and impotence in return; Dari Hamis al-Dari, leader chosen by local tribes, says he and constituents are increasingly frustrated; photos; Dari, who has lived in Europe and speaks excellent English, maintained his independence during Hussein rule, shunning material blandishments; welcomes Americans and works closely with military commanders but he is losing faith, saying conditions have never been worse and warning that time is almost up for meeting expectations; Col Jeff Ingram, his partner, says Dari is caught in middle, but blames Iraqis who tear down power lines and rob each other (M)

2003-07-09.title: World Briefing: Asia
2003-07-09.abstract: CHINA: Floods Force Evacuations; TURKMENISTAN: Russian Minority Fearful;.

2003-07-09.title: Bush Presses South Africa to Step Up Efforts to Fight AIDS
2003-07-09.abstract: AIDS was one of two issues that President Bush and President Thabo Mbeki discussed. The other was Zimbabwe, which is becoming unstable.

2003-07-09.title: Bush, in Africa, Promises Aid but Offers No Troops for Liberia
2003-07-09.abstract: Pres Bush opens five-country tour of Africa with promise in Senegal to work more closely to help African countries build for future; does not commit to sending US troops to help keep peace in Liberia and repeats demand that Pres Charles Taylor step aside; goes with Senegal Pres Abdoulaye Wade to Goree Island, where slaves were sent on journey across ocean; photo; Bush does not apologize for role of US but condemns slavery in unflinching, religious terms; Sec Colin Powell and security adviser Condoleezza Rice tour island with Bush (M)

2003-07-09.title: World Briefing: Europe
2003-07-09.abstract: BELGIUM: Accord On Government Near; AZERBAIJAN: Leader Hospitalized; ICELAND: Accusations Of Sex Trafficking; ITALY: Dioxin-Tainted Cattle Seized; RUSSIA: Bomb Toll Rises To 14;.

2003-07-10.title: World Briefing | Europe: Germany: Computer Experts Sought
2003-07-10.abstract: Germany will extend green card immigration program for 18 months in effort to fill computer technology positions still vacant; more than 14,500 residence permits have been issued since program began Aug 1, 2000 (S)

2003-07-10.title: Blair Aides Don't Expect to Find Iraqi Weapons, Reports Say
2003-07-10.abstract: Senior officials in Prime Minister Tony Blair's government are reported to have said that they no longer believe weapons of mass destruction will be found in Iraq.

2003-07-10.title: Iran 'Willing to Cooperate' and Still Talking
2003-07-10.abstract: Iranian government says it is still negotiating with Dr Mohamed ElBaradei, director of United Nations' nuclear agency, before deciding whether to allow tougher inspections; ElBaradei has met with Foreign Min Kamal Kharrazi, Pres Mohammad Khatami and Gholam Reza Aghazadeh, director of Iran's nuclear agency, to press for more rigorous inspections (M)

2003-07-10.title: Whispers Of Hussein: Footloose With 7 Masks
2003-07-10.abstract: Iraq is rife with rumors about Saddam Hussein's whereabouts, and some of these border on parables (S)

2003-07-10.title: U.S. Arrests 2 Ex-Officials Who Served Hussein
2003-07-10.abstract: American military officials announce arrests of Mahmud Dhiyab al-Ahmad, former interior minister, and Mizban Kahdr al-Hadi, former Baath Party official--two top officials in former President Saddam Hussein government; 34 of 55 people on most-wanted list are now in custody; American administrator L Paul Bremer 3d has announced $25 million reward for information leading to capture of Hussein, saying fact that he is not yet in custody appears to be helping to fuel intensifying attacks against American soldiers in recent weeks; photo (M)

2003-07-11.title: Veto power on some cultural matters : Paris wins amendment in new EU constitution
2003-07-11.abstract: BRUSSELS:—  After months of strenuous lobbying by France, the leaders of Europe's constitutional convention inserted a last-minute amendment that gives countries qualified veto power on some cultural matters.

2003-07-11.title: U.S. Blocks Libya's Attempt to Gain Security Council Seat
2003-07-11.abstract: Libya agrees to let Algeria serve in its place as non-permanent member of Security Council for 2004-05 term; decision is victory for United States, which in anticipation of Libya's candidacy had encouraged introduction of rival candidates to scuttle Libya's chances of success (M)

2003-07-11.title: Sharon Heading to Britain and U.S. for Talks on 'Road Map'
2003-07-11.abstract: In the latest round of talks, the Palestinian security chief demanded the release of more than 5,000 prisoners held by Israel.

2003-07-11.title: Milosevic Prosecutors Garner New Evidence
2003-07-11.abstract: United Nations court on war crimes in former Yugoslavia has several senior Serbian suspects in custody, but whether these men will link Slobodan Milosevic to crimes such as massacre of thousands of Bosnian Muslims at Srebrenica in 1995 remains unclear; two of four Serbian military officers on trial on charges of taking part in Srebrenica massacre have unexpectedly pleaded guilty and agreed to cooperate with prosecution; that could produce most complete picture yet of who ordered massacre and to what extent Serbia itself was involved; prosecutors learn that Serbia, which has stalled for more than year, has approved handing over large cache of classified documents that might prove vital in trial of Milosevic; prosecutors are trying to prove 66 counts of crimes involving three wars, including charges of genocide; photos (M)

2003-07-11.title: Berlin Journal; Tacky Marxist Edifice to Bow to Hohenzollerns
2003-07-11.abstract: Palace of the Republic, built in early 1950's as East Berlin's entertainment showcase, then closed in 1990 after reunification of Germany, is to be razed; it will be replaced by copy of what was there before--palace of Hohenzollerns, rulers of Prussia, that was dynamited by East German government; when plan will be carried out is open question, because city of Berlin is broke and money for demolition and reconstruction is not in coffers; photos (M)

2003-07-12.title: World Briefing: Asia
2003-07-12.abstract: INDIA-PAKISTAN BUS SERVICE RESUMES; HONG KONG: Bus Crash Kills 21;.

2003-07-12.title: Names of the Dead
2003-07-12.abstract: List of Americans newly confirmed dead in Iraq

2003-07-12.title: World Briefing: Europe
2003-07-12.abstract: GERMANY: Another Chapter In Tourist War; BRITAIN: Marriage For Transsexuals; CZECH REPUBLIC: Army To Be Halved; UKRAINE: Joint Memorial To Massacre; IRELAND: A Sunken Link To Oliver Cromwell; FRANCE: Corsican Separatists Sentenced;.

2003-07-12.title: World Briefing | Asia: Hong Kong: Bus Crash Kills 21
2003-07-12.abstract: Double-decker bus collides with truck on mountain road in Hong Kong and careens down cliff; 21 people are killed and 20 injured (S)

2003-07-12.title: Russian Authorities Search Office of Powerful Oil Tycoon
2003-07-12.abstract: Russian prosecutors step up criminal investigation of oil tycoon Mikhail B Khodorovsky, sending police, some in ski masks, to search offices of Yukos Oil Co (S)

2003-07-13.title: 'Honor Killings' Defy Turkish Efforts to End Them
2003-07-13.abstract: Cemse Allak, who died of injuries seven months after being stoned for being pregnant by man who was not her husband, is buried in June, just two days before Turkey's Parliament approved sweeping human rights law that abolished provision that often reduced prison terms for killings committed in name of 'family honor'; Allak's death underscores distance between legislative pronouncements and sometimes grim realities of everyday life; women who cared for Allak as she lay dying saw to it that she had coffin and flouted Islamic law by carrying it into municipal cemetery themselves; Allak's brother Mehmet and other relatives are charged with murder of Hila Acil, who impregnated Cemse; photos; map (M)

2003-07-13.title: Israel Calls Arafat Obstacle to Peace Effort
2003-07-13.abstract: Israeli government tries to focus international blame on Yasir Arafat for setbacks in peace efforts; urges European leaders to isolate Arafat and warns that it may eventually arrest or deport him; allies of Palestinian Prime Min Mahmoud Abbas say Israel must advance peace efforts by making concessions, like releasing large numbers of prisoners; Israeli Prime Min Ariel Sharon says he is preparing to release 300 prisoners (M)

2003-07-13.title: Rumsfeld Warns of More Attacks in Iraq
Chechen Who Recruited Women Is Killed
2003-07-13.abstract: Russian forces in Chechnya kill Chechen fighter Aslan Gasayev, who recruited women for suicide bombing missions, and three bodyguards (S)

2003-07-13.title: After Protest, Hong Kong Refuses to Speed Democratic Reforms
2003-07-13.abstract: Despite demonstrators' calls for democracy, the Hong Kong government said it would not speed its four-year plan to review constitutional changes.

2003-07-14.title: Back From Africa: Bush's Promises Will Be Watched
2003-07-14.abstract: News analysis: Pres Bush made many promises to Africa during his visit there, but it is hard to assess depth of his commitment; his trip had dutiful, check-off-box quality that suggests limit to his passion; he did not visit single rural village or urban slum; question is raised about whether trip was really about domestic politics, about appealing to black voters or--more likely--to moderate white voters, especially women, who might respond to message of compassion (M)

2003-07-14.title: Mission impossible
2003-07-14.abstract: What the Americans want from their guest, Joschka Fischer, is German troops for Iraq and restraint in the dispute surrounding questionable justification for the war.

2003-07-14.title: Global Crossing founder objects to cards bearing his likeness : An executive order:Cut the deck
2003-07-14.abstract: Some people, it seems, just don't have a sense of humor. Take the lawyers representing Gary Winnick, the embattled founder of Global Crossing. Winnick started the world-spanning network company in 1997, only to ride it into bankruptcy five years later when it filed the sixth-largest Chapter 11 case in U.S. history in January 2002.

2003-07-14.title: Fujimori May Be Disgraced, but to Many in Peru He Looks Good in Comparison
2003-07-14.abstract: Many Peruvians are unhappy with President Alejandro Toledo and suggest bringing back country's thoroughly disgraced former President Alberto K Fujimori; they recall Fujimori as being strong and decisive, while they see Toledo as weak, disorganized and leading Peru toward economic calamity; Toledo's approval rating has fallen to 11 percent, with majority saying he has failed to deliver on promises of better life; Fujimori has lived in exile in Japan since 2000; if he returns to Peru, he could be tried on charges ranging from embezzlement to treason; photo (M)

2003-07-14.title: U.S. Seeks Help With Iraq Costs, But Donors Want a Larger Say
2003-07-14.abstract: United States calls for international conference in Oct to be attended by dozens of nations--many of which opposed war to oust Saddam Hussein--to raise billions of dollars to restore Iraq's economy; Bush administration is running into now familiar diplomatic problem: potential donor nations uneasy about financing military occupation; some American officials concede there will have to be more participation by other countries in deciding how money is raised and spent; Germany and France, which opposed war, say they are ready now to help rebuild Iraq but they want different structure for international aid; in response to donor concerns, American officials are pressing for creation of another element for occupation bureaucracy, trust fund for donations by other countries; it is not clear whether such a fund will be seen by donors as sufficiently independent; photo (M)

2003-07-15.title: Arafat and Abbas Agree On Roles in the Peace Effort
2003-07-15.abstract: Yasir Arafat and Palestinian Prime Min Mahmoud Abbas work out power-sharing agreement that guarantees Arafat continued influence over negotiations with Israel and over Palestinian security forces, and puts to rest threat by Abbas to resign; agreement between Arafat and Abbas said to reflect fact that two need each other: Abbas lacks Arafat's broad domestic support, while Arafat lacks Abbas's credibility with Israel and United States; Prime Min Ariel Sharon visits Prime Min Tony Blair in London in unsuccessful effort to persuade Blair's government to cut all ties to Arafat; Israelis have also failed to persuade other parties to new peace plan--Russia, European Union and United Nations--to adopt American and Israeli policy of isolating Arafat; photo (M)

2003-07-15.title: NORTH KOREA SAYS IT HAS MADE FUEL FOR ATOM BOMBS
2003-07-15.abstract: North Korean officials tell Bush administration that they have finished producing enough plutonium to make half-dozen nuclear bombs and that they intend to move ahead quickly to turn material into weapons; American intelligence agencies scramble to determine if this is bluff or if government of Kim Jong Il has really succeeded in producing material undetected; most recent tests suggest nuclear work has accelerated, but results are inconclusive; more test results are expected this week; North Korea's declaration, if true, would pose direct challenge to Pres Bush, who said two months ago that nuclear-armed North Korea 'will not be tolerated'; he decided early this year that it is too risky to take military action against main nuclear reprocessing plant at Yongbyon; now he must decide whether to negotiate with North--under its implicit nuclear threat--or hold fast to his insistence that any talks must include other regional nations, and that nuclear blackmail would be met with increasingly harsh sanctions; Bush is resisting bilateral talks for fear that Asian nations would press US to reach deal similar to one Clinton administration signed--'freeze' on nuclear activity in return for aid (M)

2003-07-15.title: World Briefing: Middle East
2003-07-15.abstract: KUWAIT: Leadership Shift; IRAN: Journalist Arrested;.

2003-07-15.title: World Briefing: Europe
2003-07-15.abstract: ITALY: Drought Aid Sought; CYPRUS: An Official Yes To The E.U.; POLAND: New Wrapping For Stalin'S Gift;.

2003-07-15.title: World Briefing | Asia: China: Catching A Fast Train
2003-07-15.abstract: Economic Observer, China's state news media, reports government is poised to purchase Japan's Shinkansen bullet train to link Beijing and Shanghai; high-speed rail technology will cost estimated $12 billion (S)

2003-07-16.title: Pentagon Sees Possible Delay In Return of the Third Division
2003-07-16.abstract: US military officials say they are committed to bringing home Third Infantry Division's troops, who led capture of Baghdad, by autumn, but possibly not by September date given to Senate by Sec Donald Rumsfeld; Marine units, not usually used to occupy territory, may be assigned to Iraq (M)

2003-07-16.title: Dispute on Iraq Weapons Clouds Blair's Trip to U.S.
2003-07-16.abstract: Prime Minister Tony Blair travels to Washington on Thursday on a mission to underline Britain's relationship with the U.S.

2003-07-16.title: Europe Weighs Helping Out in Iraq, but Under Its Own Terms
2003-07-16.abstract: European Union tentatively offers to help finance Iraq reconstruction, despite bruising disagreements with US, provided money is administered by international agency like World Bank or UN; Comr Christopher Patten says money will not be turned over to US-led authorities (M)

2003-07-16.title: World Briefing: Asia
2003-07-16.abstract: NORTH KOREA: New Search For Missing G.I.'S; MYANMAR: Pressure From U.S.; PHILIPPINES: Ruling On Marcos Money; AFGHANISTAN: 5 Killed In Attack;.

2003-07-16.title: World Briefing | Europe: Britain: Party Crasher Avoids Charges
2003-07-16.abstract: 'Comedic terrorist' Aaron Barschak, who last month crashed Prince William's 21st birthday party at Windsor Castle, will not be charged for his actions, but palace security commander has been removed from his post and some security staff are under investigation; photo (S)

2003-07-17.title: Machine-Gun Fire Is Traded at Korean Border
2003-07-17.abstract: The episode raised the question of whether the North had precipitated the episode as a dramatic reminder of its military presence.

2003-07-17.title: John Gerhart, 59, Expert On Agriculture in Africa
2003-07-17.abstract: John D Gerhart, specialist in African agriculture and land use for Ford Foundation and president of American University in Cairo until his retirement in 2002, dies at age 59; photo (M)

2003-07-17.title: Pattani Journal; Snug Hotels for Birds, Who Pay With Their Nests
2003-07-17.abstract: Tens of thousands of birds--finger-sized swifts--have been building their nests in Pattani, Thailand, for last three years; residents of Pattani are building alternative homes for them; they hope to sell their nests, which are important ingredient in expensive Asian delicacy, bird's nest soup; photos; map; recipe for bird's nest soup (M)

2003-07-17.title: World Briefing | Europe: Sicily: U.S. Military Craft Crashes
2003-07-17.abstract: Italian police report that American military helicopter from base at Sigonella (Sicily) has crashed killing all four crew members; cause is unknown (S)

2003-07-17.title: Global AIDS Fund Finding Few Answers to Its Cash Shortage
2003-07-17.abstract: Representatives of countries contributing to fund to support global struggle against AIDS and other diseases meet in Paris but make little progress toward easing cash crisis expected later this year; United Nations Secretary of Health and Human Services Tommy G Thompson says fund needs $3 billion by end of 2004; appropriations are bogged down in Washington and in European Union; governments are being upstaged by private donors; Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation has announced it would pay $100 million into fund immediately (M)

2003-07-18.title: World Briefing | Africa: Zimbabwe: Churches Regret Inaction
2003-07-18.abstract: Zimbabwe's Council of Churches apologizes for inability to stop political violence and economic collapse in Zimbabwe, which has reached epic proportions since President Robert Mugabe won contested election last year; promises to work harder for government reforms (S)

2003-07-18.title: Lonely at the Top
2003-07-18.abstract: Tepid international support for the U.S. campaign to topple Saddam Hussein has led to larger American deployment in Iraq.

2003-07-18.title: New Warning Was Put Off On Weapons Syria Plans
2003-07-18.abstract: Central Intelligence Agency and other agencies reportedly block Bush administration plan to deliver sharp new warnings about Syria's efforts to develop unconventional weapons; State Dept Under Sec John R Bolton's appearance before Congress is postponed, reportedly because he planned to say in classified portion of his testimony that Syria's development of chemical and biological weapons has progressed to point that it poses threat to stability in Middle East; government officials say intelligence agencies are being particularly rigorous in scrutinizing testimony that Bolton prepared about Syria because of controversy surrounding Pres Bush's use of disputed intelligence about Iraq's suspected nuclear weapons program (M)

2003-07-18.title: Blair's Words: 'Our Job Is to Be There With You'
2003-07-18.abstract: Excerpts from Prime Min Tony Blair's speech to joint meeting of Congress (M)

2003-07-18.title: Shutting Doors in Syria May Not End Militants' Attacks
2003-07-18.abstract: Palestinian leaders claim they have closed headquarters of at least 10 militant groups bent on waging war against Israel; in Syria, two largest militant groups, Hamas and Islamic Jihad, have closed their doors, but it is not apparent that they have ceased activities that prompted American demands in first place; Palestinian leaders say most of senior commanders of groups are still in Syria, or planning to return soon; American officials regard closing of offices in Syria as important test of willingness of Syrian leaders to modify their historic hostility toward Israel; photos (M)

2003-07-19.title: German Official Says Europe Must Be U.S. Friend, Not Rival
2003-07-19.abstract: Foreign Min Joschka Fischer of Germany, leading candidate to become European Union's first foreign minister under its new constitution, says in Washington that strong future Europe must not be developed in rivalry with US, as French government sees it; says 'we must bring all our capabilities together to win the peace' in Iraq and restates German offer of relief effort and economic help in Iraq (M)

2003-07-19.title: White House Tells How Bush Came to Talk of Iraq Uranium
2003-07-19.abstract: White House sets out its most detailed explanation yet of how disputed intelligence about Iraq's weapons program made it into Pres Bush's State of the Union address; say White House changed initial draft of speech to make it more credible by attributing assertion that Iraq had been trying to acquire uranium from Niger to public British intelligence dossier; says change was made after internal White House deliberations about best way to present information and not, as intelligence officials have said, in response to concerns raised by CIA about credibility of intelligence reports; declassifies part of its main prewar intelligence summary on Iraq's weapons programs (M)

2003-07-19.title: World Briefing: Africa
2003-07-19.abstract: CONGO: Troubles For New Government; ZIMBABWE: Court To Hear Election Challenge; SOUTH AFRICA: Happy Birthday!;.

2003-07-19.title: A Hip-Hop Fashion Bridge Across the Atlantic
2003-07-19.abstract: Mohamed Dia, who grew up in rough Paris suburb, son of parents who immigrated to France from Mali, builds successful hip-hop clothing business and escapes poverty the America way; says there is more racial integration in US than in France and more opportunities for those who strive; plans to expand business to US; photo (M)

2003-07-19.title: British Arms Expert at Center of Dispute on Iraq Data Is Found Dead, His Wife Says
2003-07-19.abstract: Dr David Kelly, arms expert at center of dispute about BBC charge that British government doctored its intelligence reports on Iraq's weapons programs to gain public support for going to war, is found dead near his home in Oxfordshire, a suicide, according to his wife; wife, Jan Kelly, says her husband was under enormous stress; Alastair Campbell, aide to Prime Min Tony Blair, has conducted wide-ranging campaign against BBC, alleging that it has let its vaunted standards of impartiality lapse in pursuit of what he has called 'agenda against the war'; Kelly, soft-spoken civil servant, may have unwittingly become caught up in painfully public political storm for which his experience as respected expert on bioterrorism and his personal life as intensely private family man had not prepared him; photo (M)

2003-07-20.title: On Eve of Crucial Week of Diplomacy, Abbas Sums Up Vision for a Palestinian State
2003-07-20.abstract: Interview with Palestinian Prime Min Mahmoud Abbas, who faces crucial week of diplomacy, bracketed by meetings with Pres Bush and Israeli Pres Ariel Sharon; in meeting with Sharon, Abbas is expected to demand Israeli pullout from at least two more Palestinian cities, and in meeting with Bush, he will request additional financial aid; Abbas, who remains unpopular among Palestinians, says it is Palestinians' interest to halt violence, respect Israelis as neighbors and pursue state only in West Bank and Gaza; expands on his vision for Palestinian state; photo (M)

2003-07-20.title: Crackdown on Tycoon Shakes Up Moscow
2003-07-20.abstract: A noisy political brawl between President Vladimir V. Putin and Mikhail B. Khodorkovsky, the country's richest man, is rewriting the rules of Russian politics.

2003-07-20.title: Rebels Push Toward the Heart of the Capital in Liberia
2003-07-20.abstract: Rebels advance swiftly into Monrovia, Liberia, shelling buildings, firing bullets and sending streams of civilians searching for safety where there is none; fighting continues for control of two bridges leading to city's main commercial artery; injured trickle into makeshift clinics run by international aid groups; rebels are frustrated with Pres Charles Taylor's promises to step aside and Pres Bush's so-far unfulfilled pledge to play active role in bringing stability; Taylor is urging his fighters to defend city and vows to remain until peacekeepers arrive; accuses US of failing to assist him in providing adequate protection for people; American Amb John W Blaney calls on rebels to stop advancing and participate in peace talks in Ghana; angry civilians, who feel they have nothing left to lose, are confronting government militias and demanding peace and end to looting; photo (M)

2003-07-20.title: Wolfowitz Visits Mass Graveyard of Hussein's Victims and Promises Help in Hunting Killers
2003-07-20.abstract: Deputy Defense Sec Paul Wolfowitz visits mass gravesite in Hilla, one of 62 such sites that American and allied investigators have discovered in southern Iraq; about 3,000 bodies have been unearthed, but townspeople say thousands more probably decomposed in shallow water table over last decade; Wolfowitz promises group of local Iraqis that occupying forces will lend whatever aid is ncessary to track down killers, but Iraqis say Americans have already squandered opportunities to capture suspected executioners and want them to do more (M)

2003-07-20.title: New U.N. Team Enters Iran
2003-07-20.abstract: Team of International Atomic Energy Agency representatives begins new round of inspections of Iran's nuclear sites (S)

2003-07-21.title: U.S. Is Creating An Iraqi Militia To Relieve G.I.'s
2003-07-21.abstract: United States is creating new Iraqi civil defense force in next 45 days that is intended to free up thousands of American troops for anti-guerrilla missions and put Iraqi face on occupation's postwar security efforts; goal is to field 7,000 American-trained militiamen to protect supply convoys and replace American troops now guarding power plants and ammunition depots; plan has strong support of new Iraqi Governing Council, and goes well beyond proposal under consideration at Pentagon to hire private contractors to provide security at sites around country; Deputy Defense Sec Paul D Wolfowitz, on visit to Iraq, says recruiting Iraqis for security and intelligence tasks is essential for rebuilding of Iraq to succeed; Gen John P Abizaid, new head of US Central Command, concurs; violence in Iraq continues; in latest incidents, two soldiers are killed and one is wounded in ambush near Mosul, and Iraqi driver for United Nations agency dies when convoy is attacked near Baghdad; American administrator L Paul Bremer 3d claims armed resistance to American-led occupation comes from small number of 'professional killers' drawn from remnants of old regime and poses 'no strategic threat' to Americans; cites signs of progress, including restoration of basic services, economic reforms and establishment of Iraqi Governing Council; photos (L)

2003-07-21.title: Curfew's Gone, but Troops Still Hem In Hebron Family
2003-07-21.abstract: More than 80 members of extended Palestinian family live in two large stone homes inside walled property in Hebron on West Bank; for them, Middle East peace initiative has brought hope, but no real confidence, that some normalcy might return to their constricted lives; Hebron, where 450 Jewish settlers live in few small enclaves among some 150,000 Palestinians, illustrates how complicted it will be to move forward with peace plan when mutual suspicion runs so deep; under previous agreement, Israel is permitted to keep troops in center of Hebron to guard settlers, even if soldiers leave other parts of town; this allows military to maintain strict control over 30,000 Palestinians; Palestinians complain of not being able to move about and of continued curfews; photos; map (M)

2003-07-21.title: In Shiite Holy City of Najaf, A Sudden Anti-U.S. Storm
2003-07-21.abstract: Protest erupts in Shiite holy city of Najaf after cleric Moktada al-Sadr claims that American forces have encircled his home, bent on arresting him for his incendiary sermon rejecting American-appointed Governing Council; he is scion of clan of beloved clerics; Lt Col Christopher C Conlin, commanding officer in Najaf, calls matter deliberate misunderstanding; says helicopters heard overhead and extra troops on streets are to provide security for visit by Deputy Defense Sec Paul D Wolfowitz; situation underscores point made by review of Iraq reconstruction effort by panel from Center for Strategic and International Studies, that lack of Iraqis involved in reconstruction at all levels, widespread unemployment and woefully inadequate means of communicating what is happening have combined to fuel ever-higher level of frustation and anger about American presence; photos; map (M)

2003-07-21.title: London Journal; What's in a Name? In This Case, Fancy Sandwiches
2003-07-21.abstract: Family of Britain's 11th Earl of Sandwich has set up sandwich-selling business; Lord Sandwich is one of few hereditary peers who won right to keep their seats in House of Lords after most were evicted by government in 1999; idea for sandwich company was born in 1992 when Lord Sandwich's second son, Orlando Montagu, stumbled on snack bar that called itself Earl of Sandwich and used family as its theme; photo (M)

2003-07-21.title: OFFICIALS DEBATE WHETHER TO SEEK A BIGGER MILITARY
2003-07-21.abstract: Capitol Hill and Pentagon debate question of whether military needs more troops worldwide; Defense Sec Donald H Rumsfeld is considering how to assign enough soldiers to fill long-term mission of stabilizing Iraq while simultaneously fulfiling other overseas commitments and providing security against terrorism at home and abroad; of Army's 33 active-duty combat brigades, only three are described as free now for new mission; 21 brigades are now assigned overseas--16 in Iraq; of those not abroad, most are already earmarked as replacement forces for other missions, like one in Afghanistan, or are building their ranks or on emergency standby in case of crisis with North Korea; officials say National Guard and Reserves, which have 201,099 members on active duty, would probably have to shoulder some of burden of any additional missions as well; Marine Corps could be asked to share long-term peacekeeping duties, which traditionally have fallen to Army; on Capitol Hill, Sen James M Inhofe, Republican, and Sen Jack Reedm, Democrat, predict that Congress will support request to expand military's personnel roster, even with growing budget deficit; photo (M)

2003-07-22.title: Bucharest Journal; On Dracula's Terrain, an Infusion of New Blood
2003-07-22.abstract: Bucharest Journal on Romanian movie industry's hopes of luring Hollywood filmmakers from Prague with offers of lower labor costs, inexpensive wood for sets and stunning landscapes; biggest undertaking so far has been Cold Mountain, Civil War epic directed by Anthony Minghella, with everything from artificial snow and blood imported; American producer Michael Flannigan and director Allan A Goldstein comment on their low-budget movie Fire; photos (M)

2003-07-22.title: White House Remains Noncommittal on Troops for Liberia
2003-07-22.abstract: The White House refused today to say when, or if, the United States would send a peacekeeping force to Liberia.

2003-07-22.title: ENRAGED LIBERIANS CALL FOR U.S. HELP AS REBELS ATTACK
2003-07-22.abstract: US marines arrive by helicopter to protect US Embassy in Monrovia and help evacuate foreigners in midst of heavy shelling by rebel forces; hundreds of enraged Liberians lay mutilated bodies by embassy's gates, demanding that US intervene; photos; map; mortar rounds pound neighborhood near embassy, one shell dropping on grounds (M)

2003-07-22.title: World Briefing | Middle East: Egypt: Court Acquits 11
2003-07-22.abstract: Eleven Egyptian men accused of homosexual activity are aquitted, but labeled sinners by court; international human rights groups criticized similar case on March 21 in which homosexuals were sentenced by Egyptian court to three years in prison (S)

2003-07-22.title: Iraq's Governing Council Gains Support at U.N.
2003-07-22.abstract: The United Nations' special representative to Iraq gave firm support to the governing council in Baghdad today.

2003-07-23.title: Hussein's 2 Sons Dead in Shootout, U.S. Says
2003-07-23.abstract: US troops, acting on tip from Iraqi, surround house in Mosul and kill Saddam Hussein's sons Uday and Qusay in ferocious shootout after men inside refuse to surrender; photos; map; bodies are flown to Baghdad and identified; Gen Ricardo Sanchez says they were physically recognizable and 'multiple sources' were used to confirm identities; two other bodies taken from house may be Qusay's teenage son Mustapha and Uday's bodyguard; retired Iraqi general living nearby says others may have escaped; Sanchez says $15-million reward for each of sons will be paid, possibly to house's owner who neighbors say was seen sitting calmly in US vehicles near scene; US soldier is killed and second wounded in ambush north of Baghdad; Red Cross reports technician killed near Hilla (M)

2003-07-23.title: Hotel bombings tied to Basque separatists : Two blasts wound 13 in Spain
2003-07-23.abstract: MADRID:—  Two bombs planted in beachfront hotels on Spain's Mediterranean coast exploded Tuesday, wounding 13 people, including foreign students, tourists and police officers.

2003-07-23.title: World Briefing | Middle East: Iran: Report On Journalist
2003-07-23.abstract: Jomhouri Eslami, Iranian hard-line newspaper, infers that Iranian-Canadian photojournalist Zahra Kazemi, who died while in custody from brain hemorrhage, purposely slammed her head into wall in effort to frame her captors for maltreatment; hard-line Judge Saeed Mortazavi, who participated in Kazemi's interrogation, has recently been appointed to investigate her death (S)

2003-07-23.title: Iraqis Wait to See U.S. Proof That Hussein Sons Are Dead
2003-07-23.abstract: The U.S. holds irrefutable proof that Saddam Hussein's sons are dead, but the Iraqi public held out today for photographs.

2003-07-23.title: Allegations range from simple bribes to stock swindles : Music scandal rocks Hong Kong
2003-07-23.abstract: HONG KONG:—  A widening music industry scandal has transformed Hong Kong's top of the pops into a Ten Most Wanted list.

2003-07-24.title: Surabaya Journal; For These Transvestites, Still More Role Changes
2003-07-24.abstract: Transvestites go back many centuries in Indonesia; their recognition stands in contrast to relative disapproval of homosexuality; now country's transvestites are trying to create roles for themselves beyond their long tolerated positions as television stars, entertainers and beauticians, moving even into political arena; in keeping with Indonesia's mostly relaxed practice of Islam, imams appear to be tolerant of transvestites; photo; map (M)

2003-07-24.title: U.S. Defends Move to Storm House Where Hussein Brothers Were Hiding
2003-07-24.abstract: Military commanders in Iraq and Pentagon officials in Washington defend decision to storm house in Mosul where Saddam Hussein's sons were hiding rather than try to encircle it and force them to surrender; Defense Sec Donald H Rumsfeld says sons were 'determined to fight to the death'; Pres Bush says their deaths are clearest sign yet that former regime is gone and will not be coming back; military officials say waiting out two sons posed several risks, including their escape through suspected tunnel to nearby buildings; say prolonged siege could have given guerrillas time to fire on 200 American forces surrounding house; photo (M)

2003-07-24.title: Allegations range from simple bribes to stock swindles : Music scandal rocks Hong Kong
2003-07-24.abstract: HONG KONG:—  A widening music industry scandal has transformed Hong Kong's top of the pops into a Ten Most Wanted list.

2003-07-24.title: Canada Recalls Envoy From Iran After Burial of Detained Reporter
2003-07-24.abstract: Canada announces it is pulling its ambassador out of Iran after Canadian freelance photographer Zahra Kazemi, who died in Iranian custody after blow to head, is buried in her birthplace in southern Iran; Canadian government had demanded that her remains be sent to Canada for autopsy and burial; Kazemi was arrested on June 23 as she took pictures of protests outside prison in Tehran, where many dissidents are jailed; Iranian government first said she suffered stroke, then later admitted she died from brain hemorrhage caused by blow to her head; Canadian Foreign Min Bill Graham warns of other sanctions being considered against Iranl photo (M)

2003-07-24.title: Blair Urges Hong Kong to Preserve Stability
2003-07-24.abstract: British Prime Min Tony Blair gives cautious endorsement to Hong Kong's democracy movement during stopover in former British colony; praises democracy advocates for keeping protests peaceful, and says he believes Chinese leaders are dedicated to Kong Kong's constitutional development and stability; some local democracy advocates are unimpressed by his caution (S)

2003-07-25.title: PENTAGON LEADERS WARN OF DANGERS FOR U.S. IN LIBERIA
2003-07-25.abstract: Top two American military officers warn of significant dangers facing United States military involvement in Liberia, two days after Secretary of State Colin L Powell calls for speedy deployment of troops to that country; Gen Richard B Myers and Gen Peter Pace, chairman and vice-chairman of Joint Chiefs of Staff, call for clear mission statement and strategy for successful end before any troops are sent; Myers says situation in Liberia does not lend itself to quick fix; Pace says if American troops are sent there, they may have to take military action; generals face insistent questioning from Sen John W Warner, chairman of Armed Services Committee, who calls situation in Liberia 'very tragic' but voices concern about sending American troops into 'volatile situation'; Congressional action is not required for Pres Bush to send in peacekeeping troops, but Warner urges him to define clear national security interests before ordering troops to Liberia; Powell, in closed session with House International Relations Committee, reiterates his support for American role, citing it as moral imperative (M)

2003-07-25.title: World Briefing | Americas: Argentina: Arrests Of Ex-Officers Ordered
2003-07-25.abstract: Argentine court issues arrest warrants for 46 individuals that were part of military dictatorship from 1976 to 1983, at request of Spanish Judge Baltasar Garzon; Argentine President Nestor Kirchner still needs to sign official decree allowing human rights violators to stand trial abroad; General Jorge Videla and Admiral Emilio Massera are among those indicted (S)

2003-07-25.title: Excerpts From Report on Intelligence Actions and the Sept. 11 Attacks
2003-07-25.abstract: Excerpts from report by Congressional intelligence committees on actions of FBI and CIA before and after Sept 11 attacks (L)

2003-07-25.title: Cheney Asserts No Responsible Leader Could Have Ignored Danger From Iraq
2003-07-25.abstract: Vice Pres Dick Cheney, in rare speech in Washington, offers White House's most forceful rebuttal to growing tide of skepticism about justifications for war in Iraq; argues it would have been 'irresponsible in the extreme' to ignore threat from Saddam Hussein's weapons program; his comments reflect effort to recast Iraq war as part of broader campaign against terror, linkage that administration officials say puts Iraq in better political light for them; Defense Sec Donald H Rumsfeld has made similar comments at recent news conference; Cheney's speech seeks to put administration's critics on defensive, saying safety of Americans was at stake; reads from four sections of recently declassified National Intelligence Estimate on Iraq's weapons programs; that report's conclusions have come under increasing scrutiny; photo (M)

2003-07-25.title: Israeli Soldier's Gunfire Kills Palestinian Child and Wounds 2
2003-07-25.abstract: An Israeli soldier fired a machine gun that killed a 4-year-old Palestinian boy today and wounded two young girls.

2003-07-26.title: Uday's Trove: To Remember or Forget?
2003-07-26.abstract: Goods from Uday Hussein's extensive estate remain in locked warehouses and storage rooms in main palace, Baghdad, that American-led alliance uses as headquarters; much of collection will be auctioned off in coming months, with proceeds used to repair damage done by Saddam Hussein's violent older son; some items may be retained for museum or documentary record of Saddam Hussein's rule; photo (M)

2003-07-26.title: Fisherman Seeks to Harvest Ailing Baltic, Gently
2003-07-26.abstract: Profile of Kurt Christensen, head of Danish Society for a Living Sea, Baltic-wide network of fishermen frustrated by region's dwindling fish stocks and near collapse of cod, ancient staple; group holds European Union's big subsidies have worsened depletion by building oversize fishing fleet; seeks ban on 'brutal fishing methods' like widespread bottom trawling that rips up sea floor; photo (M)

2003-07-26.title: Classified Section Of Sept. 11 Report Faults Saudi Rulers
2003-07-26.abstract: Still-classified section of joint report by House and Senate intelligence committees reportedly says senior Saudi Arabian officials have funneled hundreds of millions of dollars to charitable groups and other organizations that may have helped finance Sept 11 terrorist attacks; section, which focuses on role played by foreign governments, reportedly centers almost entirely on Saudi Arabia; some members of Congress resent Bush administration's refusal to let committees disclose chapter's contents, holding desire to protect ruling Saudi family prevents American public from learning key facts about attacks; photos (M)

2003-07-26.title: Bush, With Palestinian Prime Minister, Urges Compromise
2003-07-26.abstract: Pres Bush meets with Palestinian Prime Min Mahmoud Abbas at White House for first time; signals to both Palestinians and Israelis that he will push them toward tough decisions for peace; Bush suggests he backs Palestinians opposition to Israel's construction of security fence on West Bank, says Abbas must clamp down on Palestinian terrorism and rebuffs calls for Israel to release up to 6,000 prisoners, many accused of terrorism; notes United States recently provided $20 million to Palestinian Authority for economic development; says Secs John W Snow and Donald L Evans will visit region in fall to suggest specific development plans (M)

2003-07-26.title: Russian Colonel Guilty of Murder in Death of Chechen Woman
2003-07-26.abstract: Russian military court convicts Col Yuri D Budanov of murder in Mar 2000 strangling death of Chechen woman Elza Kungayeva, 18, in his quarters; he is highest-ranking officer convicted for what rights group say are routine abuses against civilians in Russia's war against Chechen rebels; was acquitted in first trial on temporary insanity grounds, but Supreme Court threw out verdict; is sentenced to 10 years in prison and stripped of rank and awards; officials announce discovery in village near Moscow of cache of five explosive devices like those used in recent wave of Chechen suicide bombings (M)

2003-07-27.title: In a Startling Plea, a Serbian Policeman Confesses to Atrocities
2003-07-27.abstract: Darko Darko Mrdja, Serbian policeman, pleads guilty in The Hague to taking part in notorious massacre of more than 200 prisoners of war in Bosnia on Aug 21, 1992; Serbian soldiers and police officer separate more than 200 men, mostly Croats and Muslims, from convoy of civilians, took their captives to wooded ravine, told them to kneel on edge of a cliff, and shot them (M)

2003-07-27.title: For Mixed-Race South Africans, Equity Is Elusive
2003-07-27.abstract: Mixed-race people in South Africa are still denied status as first-class citizens; one mixed-race South African says that under apartheid, they were not white enough, and that now they are not black enough; many mixed-race people of Cape Flats, where poverty and unemployment help push young men into violent street gangs and where life seems to have gotten harder since apartheid's end, say they feel there is no place for them in new South Africa; photos; map (M)

2003-07-27.title: Israeli Cabinet Agrees to Free 100 Militants and Lift Roadblocks
2003-07-27.abstract: The move came just hours before Prime Minister Ariel Sharon flew to the U.S., where he could face rare differences with President Bush on Mideast peacemaking.

2003-07-27.title: Brief Standoff Is a Lesson in Philippine History
2003-07-27.abstract: This weekend's unrest was only a caricature of the coups and popular putsches that have kept the country on edge for years.

2003-07-27.title: Inching Toward Democracy, Cambodia Again Visits Polls
2003-07-27.abstract: Cambodia is inching toward democracy, with 6.3 eligible voters set to cast ballots that are expected, by hook or by crook, to produce another five-year term for Prime Min Hun Sen; Hun Sun leaves campaigning to lesser figures, although he will open new schools and meet with admirers; civil society plays increasing role in national affairs; Hun Sen allows remarkably freewheeling press for self-described strongman; photos (M)

2003-07-28.title: Soldier Killed and 3 Others Wounded in Baghdad Bombing
2003-07-28.abstract: No one was detained after the attack, the latest in a series of lethal and sophisticated strikes against American soldiers.

2003-07-28.title: 3 Iraqis Killed as G.I.'s Set Up Raid in Hunt for Hussein
2003-07-28.abstract: American soldiers fire on Toyota passenger car in upscale Baghdad neighborhood, killing at least three Iraqis, as American Special Operations team prepares to raid nearby house in what Iraqi policeman later describes as unsuccessful attempt to capture Saddam Hussein; owner of home that is raided claims he has not seen Hussein for more than year; shootings outrage local residents, who say Americans did not offer adequate warnings before firing on car; photo (M)

2003-07-28.title: Brief Mutiny Was Pale Shadow of Past Philippine Coups
2003-07-28.abstract: Latest brief mutiny in Philippines is mere caricature of coups and popular putsches that have kept country on edge for past 18 years; no shots are fired and no real threats are made by either side; in latest incident, band of 300 junior military officers and troopers barricade themselves inside apartment building in Manila for 18 hours, demanding resignation of president and defense secretary and better equipment for soldiers in field; they later give up and agree to return to barracks, but their passion is real and some of their grievances--corrupotion and mismanagement in military--seem justified; some past coup attempts recalled; photo (M)

2003-07-28.title: El Quemado Journal; A Mexican Village Mourns Its Abducted Sons
2003-07-28.abstract: Residents of village of El Quemado, Mex, are haunted by memories of what happened in Sept, 1972, when soldiers surounded their fields and summoned all male residents and accused them of having helped guerrillas stage ambush that left several soldiers dead; men were tortured and close to 50 of them were convicted of treason and sentenced to jail; 7 of them were never seen again; Pres Vicente Fox had promised to investigate this and other heinous atrocities, but Human Rights Watch has faulted him for not putting his full authority behind that effort; photos; map (M)

2003-07-28.title: Japan Courts a Public Wary Of Sending Its Troops to Iraq
2003-07-28.abstract: Japanese government officials win bitterly disputed parliamentary vote to send military force to Iraq, then set out to sell skeptical public on sending troops to area where they could find themselves in combat for first time since end of World War II (M)

2003-07-29.title: Senators Assail Bush Aides for Lack of Data on Iraq's Future
2003-07-29.abstract: Senators assailed officials today for failing to give projections on the length, cost and troop levels for the postwar effort.

2003-07-29.title: Moscow Journal; Young, Russian, and Rich -- for Now at Least
2003-07-29.abstract: Children of Russia's new rich, first generation to come of age after Communism, remember little or nothing of Soviet privations; many live life of royalty, with servants and expensive educations in foreign boarding schools; their parents came into their fortunes during Russia's property grab, few short years in 1990's; downside to instant fortunes amassed during time of economic chaos is that government occasionally threatens to take wealth away, as evidenced by Kremlin's recent attack on Russia's richest man, Mikhail B Khodorkovsky; photo (M)

2003-07-29.title: Rights Group Reports Abuses by Afghans, Some Backed by U.S.
2003-07-29.abstract: New York-based Human Rights Watch report documents widespread extortion, armed robbery and kidnapping by police and intelligence officials and militias in Afghanistan, and accuses United States of supporting some of worst offenders; blames all countries for not doing enough to intervene and halt abuses; says perpetrators are people who came to power after US-led intervention that overthrew Taliban government in 2001; authors of report predict that warlords and abusive commanders would become more entrenched with time and could threaten success of Pres Hamid Karzai's government; report does not criticize Karzai; some diplomats and government officials contend that his indecisiveness and his policy of appeaseng powerful warlords to keep peace have kept many abusers in power (M)

2003-07-29.title: World Briefing | Europe: Germany: Bank Heir's Killer Jailed For Life
2003-07-29.abstract: Magnus Gaefgen is sentenced to life in prison for abduction and murder of 11-year-old Jakob von Metzler, who was heir to one of Frankfurt's (Germany) oldest banking families (S)

2003-07-29.title: World Briefing | Asia: India: 2 Die In Bombay Bus Bomb
2003-07-29.abstract: Bus bombing in Bombay (India) kills 2 people and injures 31 others; local authorities blame terrorist group (S)

2003-07-30.title: Thai Vice King's Lament: Police Don't Stay Bought
2003-07-30.abstract: Article on netherworld of black economy in Thailand, where corruption is so thoroughly entwined in public life that, if it were pulled up by roots, society might fall apart; Chuwit Kamolvisit, who owns six industrial-sized massage parlors and is proud to be known as Thailand's sex tycoon, complains loudly that after paying millions in bribes, he is still accused of criminal activities, without protection he has paid for with 'whole trays of Rolex watches'; photo; most Thais, wealthy and poor, have close personal experience of police corruption, and illegal economy is as much as one-third size of mainstream legal economy (M)

2003-07-30.title: World Briefing | Europe: Ireland: J.F.K.'S Boxers
2003-07-30.abstract: John F Kennedy's Navy-issue boxer shorts are on display in Louis Copeland's Dublin shop window after Irish businessman Paul Allen purchased them online for $5,000; boxers, along with dress shirt of Kennedy's, will be sent to John F Kennedy Library and Museum in Boston; photo (S)

2003-07-30.title: Pursuing Hussein, U.S. Captures 175 In Dozens of Raids
2003-07-30.abstract: American soldiers in Iraq conduct dozens of raids and seize more than 175 suspected Saddam Hussein loyalists in his hometown of Tikrit, in what military calls accelerating drive to flush him out of hiding; forces conduct 58 raids in 24 hours, garnering clues to Hussein's whereabouts; hunt coincides with new audiotape, apparently by Hussein, referring to deaths of sons Uday and Qusay; Iraqis seized include Hussein bodyguard Adnan Abdullah Abid al-Musslit and Tikrit security chief Daher Ziana; photo; map; Gen Richard B Myers warns Tikrit-Baghdad area is still 'war zone'; former Baghdad University dean Dr Muhammad Abdullah Falah Alrawi is shot to death in apparent revenge for his close links to Hussein; Iraqi Governing Council announces rotating chairmanship (M)

2003-07-30.title: World Briefing | Europe: Germany: Kidnapped Tourist Reported Dead
2003-07-30.abstract: German news reports German female tourist has died of heat stroke while being held hostage in Sahara Desert; 13 other European tourists, who were with her and are believed to have been taken by Algerian Islamic militants, are still missing and thought to be held in Mali (S)

2003-07-30.title: Russia Finds No Corner Is Safe From Chechnya's War
2003-07-30.abstract: Village of Tolstopaltsevo, which is in wooded outskirts of Moscow and is where Russians go to escape city, is also where authorities have unearthed cache of explosives fashioned into suicide belts, apparently for use by Chechen terrorists; photo; map; undercurrents of tension and increased security patrols since bombing at rock 'n' roll festival described (M)

2003-07-31.title: World Briefing | Asia: North Korea: End To Anti-South Broadcasts
2003-07-31.abstract: Senior North Korean official Kim Ryong Song announces Voice of National Salvation, North Korean propaganda radio station, will stop broadcasting after over 30 years of airing hostile messages targeted to South Korea (S)

2003-07-31.title: World Briefing | Europe: Britain: Mothers Lose Vaccine Appeal
2003-07-31.abstract: British Court of Appeals rules two mothers must permit their daughters to be given controversial three-in-one vaccine for measles, mumps and rubella despite study possibly linking vaccine to autism; cases were brought by fathers of girls; court cites lack of evidence against vaccine and strong evidence supporting benefits of vaccinations (S)

2003-07-31.title: In Najaf, Justice Can Be Blind but Not Female
2003-07-31.abstract: Protests in Najaf over swearing in of city's first-ever female judge is emblematic of tension between American desire to leave imprint on levers of government in Iraq versus conservative religious establishment determined to fight what it sees as military invasion dragging Western cultural norms in behind its tanks; Lt Col Christopher C Conlin, senior commanding officer in Najaf, is confronted with three fatwas--religious fiats by senior clergy--against appointment of Nidal Nasser Hussein as judge; says he views appointment of judge as civil matter and had not even considered consulting relgious authorities about woman judge; decides to postpone her swearing in; photo (M)

2003-07-31.title: U.S. Arms Diplomat Denounces North Korean Leader
2003-07-31.abstract: The diplomat gave a speech in South Korea today lambasting Kim Jong Il as "a tyrannical dictator" who presides over a country that is a "hellish nightmare."

2003-07-31.title: A Stranger in Afghanistan, Too Torn to Go Home
2003-07-31.abstract: Dozens of former prisoners of war and deserters were left behind in Afghanistan from 10-year-long Soviet occupation of country in 1980's; Ukranian government has granted amnesty for all deserters and POW's of Afghan war, and promised apartment, pension and free health care for those who return home; one such former POW, Gennady Anatolevich Tsevma, now known as Nek Muhammad, would love to return home but has balked at doing so out of concern for his Afghani wife and children; photo; map (M)
